questions.question: Why should bot mitigation systems separate detection from enforcement severity?
options: Because the same score may justify different actions by product surface, tenant tolerance, and rollout stage.
Because good detectors never need customer policy.
Because challenge actions always have zero user cost.
Because blocking is the only meaningful mitigation.
questions.explanation: Separating score from action gives safer tuning and better product fit.

questions.question: What usually makes edge bot mitigation hard operationally?
options: Latency budgets are tight, false positives are costly, and rule or model rollout mistakes can affect huge traffic volumes quickly.
POPs cannot make any local decisions.
Bots never adapt once challenged.
Customer-specific policy is always unnecessary.
questions.explanation: The challenge is operational safety at scale, not only classifier accuracy.

questions.question: Why is shadow mode valuable for new bot rules or models?
options: It lets the platform measure decision quality and false-positive risk before enforcing globally.
It guarantees the model has no bias.
It removes the need for reason codes.
It ensures attackers cannot observe any change.
questions.explanation: Shadow rollout is one of the safest ways to evaluate new detection logic.

questions.question: Which metric best signals that a new mitigation rule is hurting legitimate traffic?
options: A sharp shift in decision mix combined with lower challenge solve quality or business-success proxies.
Only total request count across all products.
The number of POPs in the fleet.
TLS handshake completion time for static assets.
questions.explanation: Legitimate-user harm usually shows up in action mix and downstream success indicators.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of relying heavily on cached local signals at the edge?
options: Lower latency and less dependency risk, but more freshness and consistency complexity.
Perfect global accuracy with no staleness risk.
No need for control-plane rollout.
Guaranteed lower false positives on every route.
questions.explanation: Cached local inputs are fast, but they can lag central intelligence or policy changes.

questions.question: Why should enforcement decisions carry reason codes even when models are involved?
options: Operators and customers still need enough explainability to debug, appeal, and tune policy safely.
Because reason codes make models unnecessary.
Because every request must expose raw training data.
Because false positives do not matter if a reason code exists.
questions.explanation: A platform cannot be safely operated if decisions are opaque.

questions.question: If a detector becomes more accurate but doubles hot-path latency, what is the strongest first reaction?
options: Re-evaluate what features must stay synchronous and what can move to cached or asynchronous enrichment.
Adopt it globally immediately because accuracy always dominates latency.
Remove all mitigations so latency stays flat.
Ignore the added latency unless customers complain publicly.
questions.explanation: At the edge, better detection is not automatically better if it breaks the latency budget.

questions.question: What hidden risk appears when customer-defined policies are highly flexible?
options: Conflicting or unsafe policy combinations can create inconsistent enforcement and larger blast radius without strong validation.
Attackers will stop adapting entirely.
Model rollout becomes unnecessary.
POP-level observability is less important.
questions.explanation: Flexible policy is useful, but it raises validation, precedence, and support complexity.
